skill.name: browsing-with-playwright
skill.description: Automate browser workflows through a local Playwright MCP server. The skill helps agents navigate pages, submit forms, capture screenshots, extract page data, and run Playwright actions when interactive browsing is required.
skill.summary: Use Playwright MCP for browser navigation, form automation, screenshots, data extraction, and UI testing tasks.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-crypto and markdown backtick patterns, but review found those are false positives from documentation text and JSON schema examples. Confirmed risks are legitimate but elevated for a browser automation skill: it starts local processes, makes HTTP MCP requests, can run arbitrary Playwright page code, and has a shell-enabled stdio transport. No evidence found of malicious exfiltration or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Enabled Stdio MCP Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The bundled MCP client supports a --stdio command string and starts it with subprocess.Popen using shell=True. This is useful for local MCP servers, but any untrusted command passed to --stdio could execute shell metacharacters or arbitrary commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly passes a command string into subprocess.Popen with shell=True. The feature is intentional, but the injection risk is real if a caller provides untrusted stdio input.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Browser JavaScript Execution Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill documents browser_evaluate and browser_run_code workflows that execute JavaScript or Playwright code in the active page. This is expected for advanced browser automation, but it can expose page data or mutate site state when used on sensitive websites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly instructs use of browser_evaluate and browser_run_code. The capability is legitimate for Playwright but carries meaningful risk on untrusted or sensitive pages.

medium_findings.title: Local Browser Server with Shared Browser Context
medium_findings.description: The start workflow launches @playwright/mcp with --shared-browser-context and the instructions say it maintains browser state across calls. Persistent browser state can retain sessions or cookies until the server is stopped.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The server launch command and the skill text both confirm shared browser state. This is a normal feature, but it requires users to stop the server and avoid unintended session reuse.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm hits occur in markdown and generated tool schema documentation. Review found no cryptographic implementation or security decision using weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are skill prose, generated MCP tool documentation, or markdown emission logic. No crypto API or hashing logic was found at the reviewed locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Localhost URLs Are Expected MCP Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to localhost MCP endpoints or example websites used in documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends data to an attacker-controlled remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are examples or local MCP server addresses in a browser automation skill. Network capability remains a risk factor, but the reviewed URLs do not show exfiltration intent.

low_findings.title: PID File and Process Cleanup Use Temporary Files
low_findings.description: The shell scripts store a server PID under /tmp and remove it during shutdown. This is a normal lifecycle pattern, but stale or tampered PID files could affect process cleanup on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The /tmp PID file behavior is explicit and low complexity. The impact is limited to local server lifecycle handling, not data theft or malware behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The stdio transport starts a caller-provided command through the shell. Only trusted commands should be passed to this option.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky API use is directly present and uses shell=True. The danger depends on whether callers provide trusted or untrusted command strings.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Executes Latest Playwright MCP Package
dangerous_patterns.description: The server script runs npx @playwright/mcp@latest, which fetches and executes the latest package version rather than a pinned release.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command clearly uses @latest. This is common for examples, but it weakens reproducibility and shifts trust to live package resolution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Forced Process Termination
dangerous_patterns.description: The shutdown script sends kill -9 to the stored PID if graceful shutdown does not complete. This is expected cleanup behavior, but it can terminate the wrong process if the PID file is stale or manipulated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process termination behavior is explicit. The risk is contextual and mainly affects local process safety rather than marketplace malware concerns.

content.value_statement: Manual browser work slows down testing, research, and data collection. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a Playwright MCP workflow for controlled navigation, interaction, screenshots, and extraction.
actual_capabilities: Starts and stops a local Playwright MCP server on a configurable port.
Navigates web pages and returns page state through accessibility snapshots.
Clicks elements, types text, fills forms, selects options, and waits for page conditions.
Captures full-page or viewport screenshots through Playwright MCP tools.
Runs JavaScript or multi-step Playwright code in the active browser page.
Lists and calls MCP tools through HTTP or stdio transports using the bundled client.
limitations: Requires Node.js, npx, Python 3, and Playwright MCP availability in the runtime environment.
Browser automation can interact with live websites and should only use approved targets.
The shared browser context can preserve session state until the server is stopped.
It does not replace static HTTP fetching when a page does not need browser execution.

use_cases.title: Test User Interface Flows
use_cases.description: Automate sign-in, form submission, navigation, and visual checks for web applications during manual or exploratory testing.

use_cases.title: Collect Page Data
use_cases.description: Visit dynamic websites, wait for content, inspect page state, and extract information that static requests cannot access.

use_cases.title: Debug Browser Behavior
use_cases.description: Open pages, capture screenshots, inspect console messages, and reproduce browser interactions during development support.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Playwright browsing skill to open the target URL, capture the current page state, and summarize what is visible.

prompt_templates.title: Submit a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright MCP to navigate to this form, identify the required fields, fill them with the provided values, submit it, and report the result.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Dynamic Content
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the browser automation workflow to load this dynamic page, wait for the relevant content, extract the requested fields, and note any missing data.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Multi-Step Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser_run_code for an atomic Playwright workflow that navigates, performs the required interactions, validates the final state, and captures a screenshot.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and report whether the checkout button is visible.
output_examples.output: The page loaded successfully. The checkout button is visible near the price section, and the screenshot confirms the expected layout.

output_examples.input: Fill the contact form with test data and confirm submission.
output_examples.output: The form fields were filled, the submit button was clicked, and the page displayed a success confirmation.

output_examples.input: Extract the visible article title, author, and publication date from a dynamic page.
output: Article title found on the rendered page.
Author and publication date were extracted from visible page text.
No hidden or authenticated content was accessed.

best_practices: Use browser_snapshot before clicking or typing so element references match the current page.
Stop the Playwright MCP server after the task when shared browser state is no longer needed.
Use browser_run_code only for trusted workflows and approved websites.
anti_patterns: Do not use the browser workflow for simple static downloads that curl or wget can handle.
Do not run actions against websites without user permission or a clear testing purpose.
Do not put secrets, passwords, or private tokens into prompts unless the environment is approved.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, page inspection, form interaction, screenshots, waits, and Playwright code execution through MCP.

faq.question: Does it need a local server?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow starts a local Playwright MCP server, usually on port 8808.

faq.question: Can it scrape dynamic pages?
faq.answer: Yes. It can load rendered pages and extract visible content after scripts and page waits complete.

faq.question: Can it preserve login sessions?
faq.answer: Yes. The shared browser context can preserve browser state while the server stays running.

faq.question: Is it safe for production sites?
faq.answer: Use it only with authorized targets. Browser automation can click, submit forms, and run page scripts.

faq.question: When should I not use it?
faq.answer: Do not use it when static HTTP fetching is enough or when interacting with unauthorized websites.
